title: Shan people

infobox.value: Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Multilingual_support_(Burmese) "Help:Multilingual support (Burmese)"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of [Burmese script](/wiki/Burmese_alphabet "Burmese alphabet").

([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆး, pronounced [[taj˥]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Shan_and_Tai_Lue "Help:IPA/Shan and Tai Lue"), or [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"): ရှမ်းလူမျိုး, pronounced [[ʃáɰ̃ lùmjó]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Burmese "Help:IPA/Burmese")), also known as the **Tai Long** (တႆးလူင်, [taj˥.loŋ˨˦]) or **Tai Yai**, are a [Tai ethnic group](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). The Shan are the biggest minority of [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") ([Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"))[[3]](#cite_note-:0-4) and primarily live in the [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State"), but also inhabit parts of [Mandalay Region](/wiki/Mandalay_Region "Mandalay Region"), [Kachin State](/wiki/Kachin_State "Kachin State"), [Kayah State](/wiki/Kayah_State "Kayah State"), [Sagaing Region](/wiki/Sagaing_Region "Sagaing Region") and [Kayin State](/wiki/Kayin_State "Kayin State"), and in adjacent regions of [China](/wiki/China "China") ([Dai people](/wiki/Dai_people "Dai people")), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Meghalaya](/wiki/Meghalaya "Meghalaya") ([Ahom people](/wiki/Ahom_people "Ahom people")), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") ([Kula people](/wiki/Kula_people "Kula people")), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").[[4]](#cite_note-5) Though no reliable census has been taken in Burma since 1935, the Shan are estimated to number 4–6 million,[[1]](#cite_note-POTWF-1) with [CIA Factbook](/wiki/CIA_Factbook "CIA Factbook") giving an estimate of five million spread throughout Myanmar[[2]](#cite_note-CIA_geos-2) which is about 10% of the overall Burmese population.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-4)[[5]](#cite_note-6)
(တႆး) in reference to themselves, which is also used in Chinese ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 傣族; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Dǎizú*).[[6]](#cite_note-7)

(ရှမ်း) is an [exonym](/wiki/Exonym_and_endonym "Exonym and endonym") from the [Burmese language](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"); the term itself was historically spelt သျှမ်း ([MLCTS](/wiki/MLC_Transcription_System "MLC Transcription System"): *hsyam:*), and is cognate with the term "[Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam")," the former name of Thailand.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-8) The term is extant to [Old Burmese](/wiki/Old_Burmese "Old Burmese"), first attested to a [Bagan era](/wiki/Pagan_kingdom "Pagan kingdom") inscription from 1120, where it referred to Tai-speaking populations east of the kingdom.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-8) "Shan" has also been borrowed into Chinese ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 掸族; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Shànzú*).

(ไทใหญ่, lit. 'Great Tai')[[8]](#cite_note-9) or *Ngiao* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): เงี้ยว) in [Tai yuan language](/wiki/Northern_thai_language "Northern thai language"). The Shan also have a number of exonyms in other minority languages, including [Pa'O](/wiki/Pa%27O_language "Pa'O language"): ဖြဝ်ꩻ, [Western Pwo Karen](/wiki/Western_Pwo_Karen_language "Western Pwo Karen language"): ၥဲၫ့, and [Mon](/wiki/Mon_language "Mon language") သေံဇၞော် [listen](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/2/24/LL-Q13349_%28mnw%29-%E5%92%BD%E9%A0%AD%E3%81%B9%E3%81%95-%E1%80%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%B6%E1%80%87%E1%81%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%AC%E1%80%BA.wav/LL-Q13349_%28mnw%29-%E5%92%BD%E9%A0%AD%E3%81%B9%E3%81%95-%E1%80%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%B6%E1%80%87%E1%81%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%AC%E1%80%BA.wav.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q13349_(mnw)-%E5%92%BD%E9%A0%AD%E3%81%B9%E3%81%95-%E1%80%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%B6%E1%80%87%E1%81%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%AC%E1%80%BA.wav "File:LL-Q13349 (mnw)-咽頭べさ-သေံဇၞော်.wav") (*seṃ jnok)*.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

or **Tai Lue** ([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆးလိုဝ်ႉ). Its traditional area is in [Xishuangbanna](/wiki/Xishuangbanna_Dai_Autonomous_Prefecture "Xishuangbanna Dai Autonomous Prefecture") ([China](/wiki/China "China")) and the eastern states.

or **Tai Khün** ([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆးၶိုၼ်), a subgroup of the Tai Yai making up the majority in the [Keng Tung](/wiki/Keng_Tung "Keng Tung") area. The former ruling family of [Kengtung State](/wiki/Kengtung_State "Kengtung State") belonged to this group.

or *Tai Neua*, ([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆးၼိူဝ်). The 'upper' or 'northern Tai'. This group lives north of the [Shweli River](/wiki/Shweli_River "Shweli River"), mostly in the area of [Dehong](/wiki/Dehong "Dehong"), China.

infobox.key: [Tai Ahom people](/w/index.php?title=Tai_Ahom_people&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tai Ahom people (page does not exist)")
infobox.value: The Tai Ahom people live in India's northeastern state of [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Arunachal Pradesh](/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh") where tradition says that they established the [Ahom kingdom](/wiki/Ahom_kingdom "Ahom kingdom"), or Mueng Doon Soon Kham, and ruled for almost 600 years (1228–1826). They now speak the [Assamese language](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language") with the [Ahom language](/wiki/Ahom_language "Ahom language") falling into disuse by the 19th century.
(lit. 'red Shan'), a Tai group living north of [Myitkyina](/wiki/Myitkyina "Myitkyina") in the Kachin / Shan State border area.[[11]](#cite_note-12)
*Taɯ* means 'under' or 'south.' This group lives in southern Shan State.
Also known as the "[Black Tai](/wiki/Tai_Dam_people "Tai Dam people")."

1889 photograph of a Shan woman

The **Shan people** ([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆး, pronounced [[taj˥]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Shan_and_Tai_Lue "Help:IPA/Shan and Tai Lue"), or [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"): ရှမ်းလူမျိုး, pronounced [[ʃáɰ̃ lùmjó]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Burmese "Help:IPA/Burmese")), also known as the **Tai Long** (တႆးလူင်, [taj˥.loŋ˨˦]) or **Tai Yai**, are a [Tai ethnic group](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). The Shan are the biggest minority of [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") ([Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"))[[3]](#cite_note-:0-4) and primarily live in the [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State"), but also inhabit parts of [Mandalay Region](/wiki/Mandalay_Region "Mandalay Region"), [Kachin State](/wiki/Kachin_State "Kachin State"), [Kayah State](/wiki/Kayah_State "Kayah State"), [Sagaing Region](/wiki/Sagaing_Region "Sagaing Region") and [Kayin State](/wiki/Kayin_State "Kayin State"), and in adjacent regions of [China](/wiki/China "China") ([Dai people](/wiki/Dai_people "Dai people")), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Meghalaya](/wiki/Meghalaya "Meghalaya") ([Ahom people](/wiki/Ahom_people "Ahom people")), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia") ([Kula people](/wiki/Kula_people "Kula people")), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").[[4]](#cite_note-5) Though no reliable census has been taken in Burma since 1935, the Shan are estimated to number 4–6 million,[[1]](#cite_note-POTWF-1) with [CIA Factbook](/wiki/CIA_Factbook "CIA Factbook") giving an estimate of five million spread throughout Myanmar[[2]](#cite_note-CIA_geos-2) which is about 10% of the overall Burmese population.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-4)[[5]](#cite_note-6)

'Shan' is a generic term for all [Tai-speaking peoples](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") within Myanmar (Burma). The capital of Shan State is [Taunggyi](/wiki/Taunggyi "Taunggyi"), the fifth-largest city in Myanmar with about 390,000 people. Other major cities include [Thibaw](/wiki/Thibaw_(Shan_State) "Thibaw (Shan State)") (Hsipaw), [Lashio](/wiki/Lashio "Lashio"), [Kengtung](/wiki/Kengtung "Kengtung") and [Tachileik](/wiki/Tachileik "Tachileik").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_people&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The Shan use the endonym **Tai** (တႆး) in reference to themselves, which is also used in Chinese ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 傣族; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Dǎizú*).[[6]](#cite_note-7)

**Shan** (ရှမ်း) is an [exonym](/wiki/Exonym_and_endonym "Exonym and endonym") from the [Burmese language](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"); the term itself was historically spelt သျှမ်း ([MLCTS](/wiki/MLC_Transcription_System "MLC Transcription System"): *hsyam:*), and is cognate with the term "[Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam")," the former name of Thailand.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-8) The term is extant to [Old Burmese](/wiki/Old_Burmese "Old Burmese"), first attested to a [Bagan era](/wiki/Pagan_kingdom "Pagan kingdom") inscription from 1120, where it referred to Tai-speaking populations east of the kingdom.[[7]](#cite_note-:1-8) "Shan" has also been borrowed into Chinese ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 掸族; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Shànzú*).

In [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"), the Shan are called **Tai Yai** (ไทใหญ่, lit. 'Great Tai')[[8]](#cite_note-9) or *Ngiao* ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): เงี้ยว) in [Tai yuan language](/wiki/Northern_thai_language "Northern thai language"). The Shan also have a number of exonyms in other minority languages, including [Pa'O](/wiki/Pa%27O_language "Pa'O language"): ဖြဝ်ꩻ, [Western Pwo Karen](/wiki/Western_Pwo_Karen_language "Western Pwo Karen language"): ၥဲၫ့, and [Mon](/wiki/Mon_language "Mon language") သေံဇၞော် [listen](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/2/24/LL-Q13349_%28mnw%29-%E5%92%BD%E9%A0%AD%E3%81%B9%E3%81%95-%E1%80%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%B6%E1%80%87%E1%81%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%AC%E1%80%BA.wav/LL-Q13349_%28mnw%29-%E5%92%BD%E9%A0%AD%E3%81%B9%E3%81%95-%E1%80%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%B6%E1%80%87%E1%81%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%AC%E1%80%BA.wav.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q13349_(mnw)-%E5%92%BD%E9%A0%AD%E3%81%B9%E3%81%95-%E1%80%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%B6%E1%80%87%E1%81%9E%E1%80%B1%E1%80%AC%E1%80%BA.wav "File:LL-Q13349 (mnw)-咽頭べさ-သေံဇၞော်.wav") (*seṃ jnok)*.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

## Sub-ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_people&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Sub-ethnicity")]

### Major subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_people&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Major subdivisions")]

The major groups of Shan people are:

1. **Tai Yai** ([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆးယႂ်ႇ) or **Thai Yai** ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ไทใหญ่); the 'Shan Proper', by far the largest group, by which all Shan people are known in the [Thai language](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language").
2. **[Tai Lü](/wiki/Tai_Lue_people "Tai Lue people")** or **Tai Lue** ([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆးလိုဝ်ႉ). Its traditional area is in [Xishuangbanna](/wiki/Xishuangbanna_Dai_Autonomous_Prefecture "Xishuangbanna Dai Autonomous Prefecture") ([China](/wiki/China "China")) and the eastern states.
3. **Tai Khuen** or **Tai Khün** ([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆးၶိုၼ်), a subgroup of the Tai Yai making up the majority in the [Keng Tung](/wiki/Keng_Tung "Keng Tung") area. The former ruling family of [Kengtung State](/wiki/Kengtung_State "Kengtung State") belonged to this group.
4. **[Tai Nüa](/wiki/Tai_N%C3%BCa_people "Tai Nüa people")** or *Tai Neua*, ([Shan](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language"): တႆးၼိူဝ်). The 'upper' or 'northern Tai'. This group lives north of the [Shweli River](/wiki/Shweli_River "Shweli River"), mostly in the area of [Dehong](/wiki/Dehong "Dehong"), China.

The speakers of Shan, Lue, Khun and Nua languages form the majority of [Dai nationality](/wiki/Dai_people "Dai people") in [China](/wiki/China "China").

### Other Tai Shan groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_people&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other Tai Shan groups")]

There are various ethnic groups designated as *Tai* throughout [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State"), [Northern Sagaing Division](/wiki/Sagaing_Region "Sagaing Region") and [Kachin State](/wiki/Kachin_State "Kachin State"). Some of these groups in fact speak [Tibeto-Burman](/wiki/Tibeto-Burman_languages "Tibeto-Burman languages") and [Mon-Khmer](/wiki/Mon_Khmer "Mon Khmer") and [Assamese languages](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language"), although they are assimilated into Shan society.[[10]](#cite_note-TK-11)

* **[Tai Ahom people](/w/index.php?title=Tai_Ahom_people&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tai Ahom people (page does not exist)")**: The Tai Ahom people live in India's northeastern state of [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam") and [Arunachal Pradesh](/wiki/Arunachal_Pradesh "Arunachal Pradesh") where tradition says that they established the [Ahom kingdom](/wiki/Ahom_kingdom "Ahom kingdom"), or Mueng Doon Soon Kham, and ruled for almost 600 years (1228–1826). They now speak the [Assamese language](/wiki/Assamese_language "Assamese language") with the [Ahom language](/wiki/Ahom_language "Ahom language") falling into disuse by the 19th century.
* **Tai Mao**, living in the area along the banks of the [Shweli River](/wiki/Shweli_River "Shweli River") (Nam Mao). Chinese Shan language is also known as (Tai) Mao, referring to the old Shan State of [Mong Mao](/wiki/Mong_Mao "Mong Mao").
* **[Tai Khamti](/wiki/Khamti_people "Khamti people")**. The Tai Khamti an [outlier](/wiki/Outlier "Outlier") group speaking the [Khamti language](/wiki/Khamti_language "Khamti language"). Traditionally they lived in the northernmost and westernmost edges of Shan-settled areas, such as [Putao-O](/wiki/Hkamti_Long "Hkamti Long"), [Kachin State](/wiki/Kachin_State "Kachin State"). Part of the Tai Khamti were once ruled by the [Mongkawng](/wiki/Mongkawng "Mongkawng") Shan.
* **[Tai Laing](/wiki/Tai_Laing_language "Tai Laing language")**, **Tai Leng**, or **Shan-ni** (lit. 'red Shan'), a Tai group living north of [Myitkyina](/wiki/Myitkyina "Myitkyina") in the Kachin / Shan State border area.[[11]](#cite_note-12)
* **Tai Ting**, a group living around the confluence of the [Ting](/wiki/Ting_River "Ting River") and [Salween](/wiki/Salween "Salween") rivers, just to the west of [Gengma County](/wiki/Gengma_County "Gengma County"), [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), China.
* **Tai Taɯ**: *Taɯ* means 'under' or 'south.' This group lives in southern Shan State.
* **Tai Nui**, a group living to the south and east of Kengtung town.
* **[Tai Phake](/wiki/Tai_Phake_people "Tai Phake people")**. Related to the Tai Khamti, this group has a significant presence in [Assam](/wiki/Assam "Assam"), India.
* **Tai Saʔ**. The Tai Saʔ speak a variety of Ngochang ([Achang](/wiki/Achang_language "Achang language")), but are part of mainstream Shan society.
* **Tai Loi**. The Tai Loi speak a [Palaungic language](/wiki/Palaungic_languages "Palaungic languages") resembling De'ang (especially the Bulei dialect of Yunnan) and Silver Palaung. They take part in mainstream Shan society.
* **[Tai Dam](/wiki/Tai_Dam_people "Tai Dam people")**: Also known as the "[Black Tai](/wiki/Tai_Dam_people "Tai Dam people")."
* **[Tai Dón](/wiki/Tai_D%C3%B3n_people "Tai Dón people")**: Also known as the "White Tai".
* **[Maingtha](/wiki/Achang_people "Achang people")**, a Shan group that speaks a Northern [Burmish language](/wiki/Burmish_language "Burmish language")[[12]](#cite_note-Sawada2017-13)

## Religion and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_people&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Religion and culture")]

The majority of Shan are [Theravada Buddhists](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada"), and [Tai folk religion](/wiki/Tai_folk_religion "Tai folk religion"). The Shan constitute one of the four main Buddhist ethnic groups in Burma; the others are the [Bamar](/wiki/Bamar "Bamar"), the [Mon](/wiki/Mon_people "Mon people") and the [Rakhine](/wiki/Rakhine_people "Rakhine people"). The Mon were the main source of early Shan Buddhism and [Shan scripts](/wiki/Shan_script "Shan script").[[13]](#cite_note-14)
Among the Shan Buddhists in both Burma and [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") are the [Zawti sect](/wiki/Tai_Zawti "Tai Zawti"), an austere sect of [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") Buddhism whose practitioners are distinguished by their use of palm leaf texts in home altars instead of Buddha images.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

Most Shan speak the [Shan language](/wiki/Shan_language "Shan language") and are bilingual in [Burmese](/wiki/Burmese_language "Burmese language"). The Shan language, spoken by about 5 or 6 million, is closely related to [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") and [Lao](/wiki/Lao_language "Lao language"), and is part of the family of [Tai languages](/wiki/Tai_languages "Tai languages").[[15]](#cite_note-16) It is spoken in [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State"), some parts of [Kachin State](/wiki/Kachin_State "Kachin State"), some parts of [Sagaing Division](/wiki/Sagaing_Division "Sagaing Division") in Burma, parts of [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan"), and in parts of northwestern Thailand, including [Mae Hong Son Province](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_Province "Mae Hong Son Province") and [Chiang Mai Province](/wiki/Chiang_Mai_Province "Chiang Mai Province").[[16]](#cite_note-17) The two major dialects differ in number of [tones](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"): [Hsenwi](/wiki/Hsenwi "Hsenwi") Shan has six tones, while [Mongnai](/wiki/Mongnai "Mongnai") Shan has five.[[17]](#cite_note-dalby-18) The [Shan alphabet](/wiki/Shan_alphabet "Shan alphabet") is an adaptation of the [Mon–Burmese script](/wiki/Mon%E2%80%93Burmese_script "Mon–Burmese script") via the [Burmese alphabet](/wiki/Burmese_alphabet "Burmese alphabet").[[17]](#cite_note-dalby-18) However, only a few Shan can read and write in their own language. Shan state is the most illiterate state with over a million illiterates in Myanmar due to lack of basic infrastructures and [long ongoing civil war](/wiki/Myanmar_conflict "Myanmar conflict").[[18]](#cite_note-19)

The Shan are traditionally [wet-rice](/wiki/Paddy_field "Paddy field") cultivators, shopkeepers, and [artisans](/wiki/Artisan "Artisan").[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_people&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Shan States](/wiki/Shan_States "Shan States")

The [Tai-Shan](/wiki/Tai_peoples "Tai peoples") people are believed to have migrated from [Yunnan](/wiki/Yunnan "Yunnan") in [China](/wiki/China "China"). The Shan are descendants of the oldest branch of the Tai-Shan, known as *Tai Luang* ('Great Tai') or Tai Yai ('Big Tai'). The Tai-Shan who migrated to the south and now inhabit modern-day [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos") and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") are known as *Tai Noi* (or *Tai Nyai*), while those in parts of northern Thailand and Laos are commonly known as *Tai Noi* ('Little Tai' – Lao spoken)[[20]](#cite_note-nisbet-21) The Shan have inhabited the [Shan Plateau](/wiki/Shan_Hills "Shan Hills") and other parts of modern-day [Burma](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") as far back as the 10th century CE. The Shan kingdom of [Mong Mao](/wiki/Mong_Mao "Mong Mao") (Muang Mao) existed as early as the 10th century CE but became a Burmese [vassal](/wiki/Vassal "Vassal") state during the reign of King [Anawrahta](/wiki/Anawrahta "Anawrahta") of [Pagan](/wiki/Pagan_Kingdom "Pagan Kingdom") (1044–1077).

After the [Pagan Kingdom](/wiki/Pagan_Kingdom "Pagan Kingdom") fell to the [Mongols](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols") in 1287, the Shan chiefs quickly gained power throughout central Burma, and founded:

| State | Peak territory | Duration | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Myinsaing](/wiki/Myinsaing_Kingdom "Myinsaing Kingdom")–[Pinya Kingdom](/wiki/Pinya_Kingdom "Pinya Kingdom") (1297–1364) | Central Burma | 1297–1364 | Founded by three Shan brothers named [Athinkhaya](/wiki/Athinkhaya "Athinkhaya"), [Yazathingyan](/wiki/Yazathingyan "Yazathingyan") and [Thihathu](/wiki/Thihathu "Thihathu"), and the minor kingdom was a predecessor to [Ava Kingdom](/wiki/Ava_Kingdom "Ava Kingdom") |
| [Sagaing Kingdom](/wiki/Sagaing_Kingdom "Sagaing Kingdom")[[21]](#cite_note-app-ava-22) | Central Burma | 1315–1364 | [Thihathu](/wiki/Thihathu "Thihathu") was a co-founder of the Myinsaing Kingdom and the founder of the Pinya Kingdom, and the minor kingdom was a predecessor to Ava Kingdom. |
| [Confederation of Shan States](/wiki/Confederation_of_Shan_States "Confederation of Shan States") | Upper Burma | 1527–1555 | A group of Shan States led by [Sawlon](/wiki/Sawlon "Sawlon"), [Saopha](/wiki/Saopha "Saopha") of [Mohnyin](/wiki/Mohnyin "Mohnyin") conquered the Ava Kingdom in 1527 and ruled Upper Burma until 1555 |
| [Shan States](/wiki/History_of_Shan_States "History of Shan States") (Princely states) | Shan States | 1215–1885, 1948–1959 | Princely Shan States |
| British Shan States / [Federated Shan States](/wiki/Federated_Shan_States "Federated Shan States") | Shan States | 1885–1922, 1922–1948 | Princely Shan States of [British Burma](/wiki/British_Burma "British Burma") were nominally sovereign [princely states](/wiki/Princely_states "Princely states"), but they were subject to British Crown.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24) |

Many [Ava](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Ava "Kingdom of Ava") and [Pegu](/wiki/Hanthawaddy_Kingdom "Hanthawaddy Kingdom") kings of Burmese history between the 13th–16th centuries were of (partial) Shan descent. The kings of Ava fought kings of Pegu for control of the [Irrawaddy](/wiki/Irrawaddy_River "Irrawaddy River") valley. Various Shan states fought Ava for the control of [Upper Burma](/wiki/Upper_Burma "Upper Burma"). The states of [Monyhin](/wiki/Mohnyin_Township "Mohnyin Township") (Mong Yang) and [Mogaung](/wiki/Mogaung "Mogaung") were the strongest of the Shan States. Monhyin-led [Confederation of Shan States](/wiki/Confederation_of_Shan_States "Confederation of Shan States") defeated Ava in 1527, and ruled all of Upper Burma until 1555.[[24]](#cite_note-hb-95-25)

The Burmese king [Bayinnaung](/wiki/Bayinnaung "Bayinnaung") conquered all of the Shan states in 1557.[[25]](#cite_note-app-108-26) Although the Shan states would become a tributary to Irrawaddy valley based Burmese kingdoms from then on, the Shan [Saophas](/wiki/Saopha "Saopha") retained a large degree of autonomy. Throughout the Burmese feudal era, Shan states supplied much manpower in the service of Burmese kings. Without Shan manpower, it would have been harder for the [Burmans](/wiki/Burmans "Burmans") alone to achieve their victories in [Lower Burma](/wiki/Lower_Burma "Lower Burma"), [Siam](/wiki/Siam "Siam"), and elsewhere. Shans were a major part of Burmese forces in the [First Anglo-Burmese War](/wiki/First_Anglo-Burmese_War "First Anglo-Burmese War") of 1824–1826, and fought valiantly—a fact even the British commanders acknowledged.[[26]](#cite_note-rlf-123-27)

In the latter half of the 19th century Shan people migrated into [Northern Thailand](/wiki/Northern_Thailand "Northern Thailand") reaching [Phrae Province](/wiki/Phrae_Province "Phrae Province").[[27]](#cite_note-28) The Shan population in Thailand is concentrated mainly in [Chiang Rai](/wiki/Chiang_Rai_city "Chiang Rai city"), [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai "Chiang Mai"), [Mae Hong Son](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son "Mae Hong Son"), [Mae Sariang](/wiki/Mae_Sariang_District "Mae Sariang District"), [Mae Sai](/wiki/Mae_Sai_District "Mae Sai District") and [Lampang](/wiki/Lampang "Lampang"), where there are groups which settled long ago and built their own communities and temples. Shan people are known as "Tai Yai" in north Thailand, where the word *Shan* is very seldom used to refer to them.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

After the [Third Anglo-Burmese War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Burmese_War "Third Anglo-Burmese War") in 1885, the [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") gained control of the [Shan states.](/wiki/Shan_States "Shan States") Under the British colonial administration, the [Shan principalities](/wiki/Shan_States "Shan States") were administered separately as British protectorates with limited monarchical powers invested in the Shan [Saophas](/wiki/Saopha "Saopha").[[29]](#cite_note-30)

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the Shan and other ethnic minority leaders negotiated with the majority [Bamar](/wiki/Bamar "Bamar") leadership at the [Panglong Conference](/wiki/Panglong_Conference "Panglong Conference"), and agreed to gain independence from Britain as part of [Union of Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma"). The Shan states were given the option to secede after 10 years of independence. The Shan states became Shan State in 1948 as part of the newly independent Burma.

General [Ne Win](/wiki/Ne_Win "Ne Win")'s [coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "Coup d'état") overthrew the democratically elected government in 1962, and abolished Shan saopha system.

A Shan independence movement has been active and engaged in armed struggle, leading to intermittent [civil war](/wiki/Civil_war "Civil war") within Burma for decades. Currently two main Shan armed insurgent forces operate within Shan State: the [Shan State Army/Special Region 3](/wiki/SSA/SR-3 "SSA/SR-3") and [Shan State Army/Restoration Council of Shan State](/wiki/SSA/RCSS "SSA/RCSS"). In 2005 the [Shan State National Army](/wiki/Shan_State_National_Army "Shan State National Army") (SSNA) was effectively abolished after its surrender to the Burmese government. Some SSNA units joined the SSA/RCSS, which has yet to sign any agreements, and is still engaged in [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") against the [Burmese Army](/wiki/Burmese_Army "Burmese Army").[[30]](#cite_note-31)

During conflicts, Shan civilians are often burned out of their villages and forced to flee into Thailand. Some of the worst fighting in recent times occurred in 2002 when the Burmese army shelled the Thai border town of [Mae Sai](/wiki/Mae_Sai_town "Mae Sai town"), south of [Tachileik](/wiki/Tachileik "Tachileik"), in an attempt to capture members of the [SSA](/wiki/Shan_State_Army "Shan State Army")'s [Southern Faction](/wiki/Shan_State_Army_-_South "Shan State Army - South") who had fled across the [Nam Ruak](/wiki/Nam_Ruak "Nam Ruak").[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) While in July of that same year, in the Shan Township of [Mong Yawng](/wiki/Mong_Yawng_Township "Mong Yawng Township"), the killing of a member of an [NGO](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") by the [Burmese Tatmadaw](/wiki/Tatmadaw "Tatmadaw"), and the subsequent closure of the border to Thailand, caused an evacuation of the surviving members across the [Mekong River](/wiki/Mekong_River "Mekong River") to [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos").[[33]](#cite_note-34) This evacuation was aided by members of the Shan State Army, and in turn brought tighter measures restricting foreign aid in the area as violence increased.

Due to the civil war, males typically find low-paid work in construction, while many Shan females fall in the hands of [human trafficking](/wiki/Human_trafficking "Human trafficking") gangs and end up in the prostitution business or bride trafficking.[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) Despite the hardships, Shan people in Thailand are conscious of their culture and seek occasions to gather in cultural events.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

A major Shan militia called the [Mong Tai Army](/wiki/Mong_Tai_Army "Mong Tai Army") had fought the [Tatmadaw](/wiki/Tatmadaw "Tatmadaw") as well as the [United Wa State Army](/wiki/United_Wa_State_Army "United Wa State Army") (UWSA) for a separate state up until their surrender in 1996. Since then, the UWSA has given support to [Shan State Army – South](/wiki/Shan_State_Army_%E2%80%93_South "Shan State Army – South") and [Shan State Army – North](/wiki/Shan_State_Army_%E2%80%93_North "Shan State Army – North") out of hope that the two serve as buffers against Tatmadaw encroachment on Wa territory.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### Communities in exile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Shan_people&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Communities in exile")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Poi_Sang_Long_Festiva04.JPG/250px-Poi_Sang_Long_Festiva04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Poi_Sang_Long_Festiva04.JPG)

Traditional Poi Sang Long [novice ordination](/wiki/%C5%9Ar%C4%81ma%E1%B9%87era "Śrāmaṇera") festival celebrated by one of the Shan communities in exile in Thailand

Following the arrest of [Sao Shwe Thaik](/wiki/Sao_Shwe_Thaik "Sao Shwe Thaik") of [Yawnghwe](/wiki/Yawnghwe "Yawnghwe") in the [Burmese coup d'état](/wiki/1962_Burmese_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1962 Burmese coup d'état") in March 1962 by the Revolutionary Council headed by General [Ne Win](/wiki/Ne_Win "Ne Win"),[[38]](#cite_note-dms-39) his wife [Sao Nang Hearn Kham](/wiki/Sao_Nang_Hearn_Kham "Sao Nang Hearn Kham") fled with her family to [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") in April 1962 and Sao Shwe Thaik died in prison in November the same year. In exile, his wife took up the cause of the independence struggle of the [Shan State](/wiki/Shan_State "Shan State"). In 1964 Sao Nang Hearn Kham with her son Chao-Tzang Yawnghwe helped to form the [Shan State War Council](/wiki/Shan_State_War_Council "Shan State War Council") (SSWC) and the [Shan State Army (SSA)](/wiki/Shan_State_Army "Shan State Army"), becoming chair of the SSWC,[[39]](#cite_note-BFP-40) and taking the Shan rebellion that started in 1958 to a new phase.[[40]](#cite_note-ms-41)
Sao Nang Hearn Kham died on 17 January 2003 in exile in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada") at the age of 86.[[39]](#cite_note-BFP-40)

The declaration of independence was rejected by most other [ethnic minority](/wiki/Ethnic_minority "Ethnic minority") groups, many Shan living inside Burma, and the country's leading opposition party, [Aung San Suu Kyi](/wiki/Aung_San_Suu_Kyi "Aung San Suu Kyi")'s [National League for Democracy](/wiki/National_League_for_Democracy "National League for Democracy"). Despite the domestic opposition to the declaration, the [Burmese Army](/wiki/Myanmar_Army "Myanmar Army") is rumoured to have used it as a reason to crack down on Shan civilians. Shan people have reported an increase in restrictions on their movements and an escalation in [Burmese Army](/wiki/Myanmar_Army "Myanmar Army") raids on Shan villages. The October 2015 Burmese military offensive in Central Shan State has displaced thousands of Shan people, as well as [Palaung](/wiki/Palaung_people "Palaung people"), [Lisu](/wiki/Lisu_people "Lisu people") and [Lahu people](/wiki/Lahu_people "Lahu people"), causing a new humanitarian crisis.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) Shan [civil society organisations](/wiki/Civil_society_organisation "Civil society organisation") are concerned about the lack of international response on the recent conflict.[[43]](#cite_note-44)
